result:
The first thing you notice about the air at 3:17 a.m. is that it has edges.  It slips between the ribs like cold wire, a reminder that the world has not yet resigned itself to the idea of human comfort.  I am walking barefoot down the middle of a street that belongs more to raccoons and sodium lamps than to any civic master-plan, and I am thinking—no, I am not thinking, I am eavesdropping on myself—about how every memory I own is actually a translation of a translation, like a photocopy of a dream of a photograph.  Somewhere back there I left my shoes on the porch because the wood was warm from the day’s sun, and the sensation of warm pine against arches felt like the last honest treaty my body would ever sign with the planet.  Now the pavement is drinking the heat out of me, and I let it.  I want to be an empty vessel for once, something echo-shaped.

There is a sound that distance makes when it is gathering.  It is not the echo of a train or the hush of tires on the interstate; it is the slow cottony hush that happens inside the skull when you realize that every room you have ever loved is still out there, still breathing its particular dust and light, and that none of them knows your name anymore.  I once lived in a third-floor apartment that smelled of cinnamon and old pianos.  The wallpaper in the hallway had a pattern of herons standing on one leg, and at night the paper herons floated off the wall and stalked across the carpet like origami ghosts.  I never told anyone this, because certain kinds of loneliness are franchised only to the living, and I was collecting franchises the way other people collect postal stamps or grievances.

Tonight the moon is a chipped enamel bowl, and it is leaking something that looks like silence but tastes like pennies.  I keep walking because motion is the only form of prayer I still trust.  Somewhere to my left a sprinkler system starts up with a cough and a hush, and the water arcs are luminous for an instant—silver vertebrae against darker dark—before they subside into ordinary hydration.  I want to tell the water:  stay miraculous, stay lit from inside, stay dangerous.  Instead I walk through the spray because I am still vain enough to believe that if something cold and clean touches me, I might become cold and clean too.  The droplets bead on my forearms, perfect lenses in which the whole street shrinks and inverts.  In one drop I see the roofline of my childhood home upside-down, and in another I see the face of a woman who once told me I had the geography of a wounded country.  I lick the water off my wrist; it tastes of iron and chlorine and the small polite deaths of a thousand suburban lawns.

We are told that memory is a palace, but mine is more like a yard sale held inside a flood.  Objects surface and drift away before I can tag them with price or story.  A plastic dinosaur missing its tail, a cassette ribbon tangled like seaweed, a single roller skate with the laces removed—each of them was indispensable once, and now they are just the flotsam that bumps against my knees when I wade through the living room at 2 p.m. on a Tuesday.  I keep waiting for a tide to carry them off, but the tide never comes; instead the water just rises an inch a year, so slowly that I mistake drowning for standing still.

I pass a house where someone is awake behind vertical blinds; the slit of light is trembling, which means either insomnia or grief.  I want to knock and offer the only currency I have left:  the fact that I too am awake, that I too have tasted the metallic midnight and survived.  But etiquette forbids it.  We are all taught that the proper response to another person’s night terror is to pretend we did not hear the plate hit the wall, the muffled 3 a.m. sob that sounds like a kettle beginning to sing.  So I keep walking, carrying my invisible suitcase of unsolicited intimacy.

At the corner I discover a snail crossing the sidewalk.  Its shell is the exact size of a punctuation mark, and the wet stripe it leaves behind is a long question without noun or verb.  I crouch, my knees popping like small fireworks, and watch.  The snail does not know it is crossing a continent of concrete; it only knows the imperative of the opposite grass.  I envy the clarity of that contract.  I want to put my thumb down in front of it, an arbitrary God, but that would be cheating.  Instead I breathe on it, a tiny weather system of carbon dioxide and secondhand melancholy.  The snail pauses, unsure whether this warm front constitutes predator or prophecy, then soldiers on.  I decide that if it reaches the far side before I count to thirty, I will survive whatever is coming.  I count slow, Mississippi-style, stretching each syllable until it becomes a suspension bridge.  At twenty-eight the snail’s front foot—if that is what you call the spatula of its body—touches the lip of grass.  I exhale, and the night releases something that feels like forgiveness, though it might just be carbon monoxide.

Standing up, I become aware that I have been talking aloud.  Not words exactly, more like phonemes loosed from their moorings:  ahh, ohhh, sss.  They hover in front of my face like small failed moons, then drift off to join the broader failure of language.  Once, in a foreign airport, I watched a man argue with a cardboard sign that did not contain his name.  He kept pointing, as if the sign might yet blush and rearrange its letters into the syllable of his belonging.  I remember thinking:  that is what all of us are doing, every day, waving at the world’s indifferent cardboard, begging it to pronounce us.  Tonight I am that man, except the street is the sign, and it refuses to name me no matter how loudly I mispronounce myself.

I turn back toward home.  The porch light is still on, a small yellow bruise against the house’s dark face.  My shoes wait like two loyal dogs, tongues out, ready to resume the fiction that the ground is something we tame rather than something that lets us borrow its spine for a little while.  I sit on the step and lift one foot.  The sole is blackened but not injured; pain, that fastidious guest, has declined to RSVP.  I cradle the foot in my hands and discover it is colder than I expected, as if it has been borrowing refrigeration from the stars.  I press it against my stomach, an ice pack against the soft repository of dinner and dread.  Slowly, the heat exchanges:  my body cools, the foot warms, and for a moment we meet in the middle, a negotiated truce, the way two countries might swap prisoners at dawn.

Inside, the house ticks with the small expanding and contracting lungs of domesticity:  the refrigerator exhales, the hot-water pipe in the wall clears its throat, the smoke detector blinks its red metronome to prove it is still vigilant.  I do not turn on any lights.  I navigate by the glow of the microwave clock—3:46, a time that does not exist in most respectable universes—and by the muscle memories of hip and shin.  In the bathroom I piss in the dark, listening to the patter against the water, a sound like applause for a performance no one asked for.  When I flush, the roar fills the small room like the inside of a shell, and I am briefly inside a sea that has forgotten its own color.

Back in bed I lie on my back, arms out, a makeshift crucifixion.  The sheet is cool, but soon it will warm to the temperature of apology.  I wait for the click that means sleep has closed its tiny metal gate behind me.  Instead I hear the snail question again:  how did we get so far across the continent of dark without knowing?  I have no answer that does not sound like a hall of mirrors apologizing to each other.  So I begin to count, not Mississippis but heartbeats.  They arrive irregularly, like postcards from a friend who is running out of foreign cities.  One hundred, one-twenty, one-forty.  Somewhere around two hundred the numbers dissolve into texture, and the texture becomes the sound of distant surf, and the surf becomes the breathing of a child who is not yet born but who has already started packing for the journey.  I open my mouth to say:  take nothing, travel lighter than light.  Instead I only yawn, and the yawn stretches wider than my body, wide enough to fit the whole yard-sale flood, the photocopied memories, the upside-down houses in the sprinkler drops, the woman who called me a wounded country.  It all fits, miraculously, inside that single dark rectangle.  Then the rectangle folds in half, and in half again, until it is the size of a postage stamp, and someone somewhere licks it and presses it onto the corner of an envelope addressed to tomorrow.  I do not know what is written on the letter inside; I only know that the stamp sticks, and that the envelope is already leaving, already lifting, already gone.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
